items.question_text: What is normally specified when transferring radio communications?

choices.text: The facility or location name and terminal function, the frequency if not omitted under a listed exception, and if required the time, fix, altitude, or specifically when to contact.
Only the new frequency, because facility names and contact timing are always optional.
Only the receiving controller's initials and the aircraft call sign.
Only the frequency and the words "change approved."

items.explanation: The paragraph lists the transfer elements: who to contact, which frequency to use unless an omission exception applies, and if required when to make contact.

items.question_text: Controllers must, within a reasonable amount of time, take appropriate action to establish or restore communications with aircraft for which a communications transfer or initial contact to their sector is expected or required.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That follow-up duty applies when expected communications do not occur.

items.question_text: If an operational advantage will be gained and coordination is completed with the receiving controller, you may instruct an aircraft on the ground to monitor the receiving controller's frequency.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Monitor instructions on the ground are allowed only when there is an operational advantage and the receiving controller has been coordinated with.

items.question_text: This frequency-change-avoidance rule applies to helicopters known to be single-piloted during air-taxiing, hovering, or low-level flight whenever necessary control instructions can be relayed until the pilot is able to change frequency.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The scope of the helicopter caution is limited to that operational context and those pilots.

items.situation: An aircraft was transferred to your sector and should have checked in by now. Five minutes have passed since the aircraft entered your area of jurisdiction and came within radio coverage. Another controller says there is no need to act yet because the transfer was already completed and the pilot will call eventually.
items.para_context: Controllers must take appropriate action within a reasonable amount of time to establish or restore communications with aircraft expected to contact their sector. A reasonable amount of time is considered 5 minutes from entry into the area of jurisdiction or coming within radio coverage.

choices.text: Take appropriate action now to establish or restore communications rather than waiting indefinitely for the pilot to call.
Do nothing until the aircraft has been silent for at least 15 minutes because the transfer itself satisfied the rule.
Assume the aircraft remained on the previous controller's frequency intentionally unless the pilot declares radio failure.
Wait for the next sector to try to contact the aircraft because the reasonable-time rule applies only to initial departure contact.

items.explanation: The paragraph creates an active follow-up duty once expected communications have not occurred within a reasonable amount of time.

items.situation: You are handing an aircraft off to Los Angeles Center on 123.4. Build the transmission.
items.para_context: When transferring radio communications, specify the facility or location name, terminal function when appropriate, and the frequency unless a listed exception allows omitting it.
items.explanation: The phrase includes the receiving facility and the frequency to use for the communications transfer.
items.target_phrase: CONTACT LOS ANGELES CENTER ONE TWO THREE POINT FOUR

items.question_text: What are the allowed verbal response types to an operational request?

choices.text: Approve it, approve it with restrictions, state unable with a reason if time permits, or state stand by.
Approve it silently, deny it only in writing, or defer it to the receiving controller.
Repeat the request back exactly and wait for the requester to decide whether it is approved.
Use only approved as requested or unable; stand by and restricted approval are not authorized responses.

items.explanation: The paragraph lists four response paths: approved, restricted approval, unable, and stand by.

items.question_text: If time permits and you cannot grant an operational request, you should state unable and provide a reason and or additional instructions as necessary.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Unable may be followed by a reason or additional instructions when time permits.

items.question_text: This operational-request response rule applies to requests from another controller, a pilot, or a vehicle operator.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The opening sentence names those three requester categories explicitly.

items.situation: A pilot requests a route amendment that you cannot approve yet because another coordination task is still pending. Another controller says you should say "approved" now so the pilot knows the request was heard and then fix the details later if needed.
items.para_context: Stand by is neither approval nor denial; it acknowledges the request and tells the requester a response will come later. Approved and unable have their own distinct meanings and uses.

choices.text: Use stand by to acknowledge the request while withholding approval until you can actually respond.
Say approved now because any acknowledgment counts as provisional approval.
Say unable permanently because a request that cannot be answered immediately must always be denied.
Remain silent until you can approve it, because stand by is considered an improper response to operational requests.

items.explanation: Stand by exists specifically to acknowledge a request without approving or denying it immediately.

items.situation: A pilot requests direct Brickyard and you can approve it as requested. Build the response.

items.question_text: When are wake turbulence procedures applied?

choices.text: To an aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required.
To every aircraft operating behind any other aircraft regardless of whether wake turbulence separation is required.
Only after the pilot reports feeling wake turbulence.
Only during visual approaches because IFR aircraft are handled by other minima rules.

items.explanation: Wake turbulence procedures are tied to situations where wake turbulence separation is required.

items.question_text: Wake turbulence separation minima must continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly carries the wake minima to touchdown for those IFR aircraft.

items.question_text: If an IFR aircraft is not making a visual approach and is not maintaining visual separation, wake turbulence separation minima must continue all the way to touchdown.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That conditional phrasing is just the rule restated in its operational trigger form.

items.question_text: This wake-turbulence rule applies to aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake separation is required and specifically addresses continuation of minima to touchdown for certain IFR aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph has both the general application statement and the touchdown-continuation rule for IFR aircraft.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft is following heavier traffic to the runway and is neither cleared for a visual approach nor maintaining visual separation. Another controller says the wake minima no longer matter once the aircraft is established on final and close to the threshold.
items.para_context: Wake turbulence procedures apply when wake separation is required, and the separation minima must continue to touchdown for IFR aircraft that are not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: Continue the required wake turbulence minima all the way to touchdown.
Drop the wake minima once the aircraft turns final because runway alignment replaces the turbulence requirement.
Convert the situation to visual separation automatically because the aircraft is near the runway.
Apply wake procedures only if the pilot specifically asks whether the preceding aircraft is heavy.

items.explanation: The touchdown requirement continues until touchdown unless the flight is in one of the stated exceptions.

items.question_text: When must wake turbulence cautionary advisories be issued to aircraft operating behind traffic requiring wake turbulence separation?

choices.text: When listed terminal or IFR visual conditions exist, and also whenever in the controller's judgment wake turbulence may have an adverse effect on an aircraft.
Only when the pilot specifically requests the advisory by name.
Only after a wake encounter has already been reported.
Only for super aircraft, because heavy-aircraft wake is covered by mandatory separation alone.

items.explanation: The paragraph gives both specified cases and a broader judgment-based caution advisory duty.

items.question_text: When traffic is known to be a Super aircraft, controllers must include the word Super in the wake turbulence cautionary advisory, and when traffic is known to be Heavy, they must include the word Heavy.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The advisory includes Super or Heavy in the traffic description when known.

items.question_text: If an IFR aircraft accepts a visual approach or visual separation behind traffic that requires wake turbulence separation, controllers must issue a wake turbulence cautionary advisory.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That IFR visual-acceptance case is one of the specifically listed advisory triggers.

items.question_text: This cautionary-advisory rule applies to specified aircraft operating behind traffic requiring wake turbulence separation and also to any aircraft that, in the controller's judgment, may be adversely affected by wake turbulence.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph includes both named trigger categories and a broader judgment-based cautionary advisory rule.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is no longer being radar vectored and is following heavier traffic. Another controller says wake caution is unnecessary because the pilot can see the aircraft ahead and vectoring has already ended.
items.para_context: Wake turbulence cautionary advisories are issued in specified cases, including terminal VFR aircraft not being radar vectored behind larger aircraft, and whenever wake turbulence may adversely affect an aircraft in the controller's judgment.

choices.text: Issue the wake turbulence cautionary advisory because the situation matches a listed advisory condition.
Omit the advisory because wake caution ends automatically when radar vectors stop.
Issue it only if the pilot first reports encountering turbulence, because cautionary advisories are reactive rather than preventive.
Replace the advisory with visual separation because wake turbulence caution can never be used for VFR aircraft.

items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly includes VFR aircraft not being radar vectored behind larger aircraft as a cautionary-advisory case.

items.situation: A heavy Boeing 747 has just departed and may affect the aircraft behind it. Build the cautionary advisory.
items.target_phrase: CAUTION WAKE TURBULENCE HEAVY BOEING SEVEN FORTY SEVEN DEPARTING OFF YOUR LEFT

items.question_text: When are traffic advisories issued to aircraft on your frequency?

choices.text: Unless the aircraft is in Class A airspace or the pilot requested omission, when in your judgment proximity may diminish below the applicable separation minima or otherwise warrants the advisory where no minima apply.
Only when a pilot explicitly asks for every traffic call regardless of airspace or proximity.
Only to IFR aircraft because VFR pilots are responsible for all traffic avoidance without advisory support.
Only when separation has already been lost and radar vectors are the next required step.

items.explanation: The paragraph states the default service, the exceptions, and the judgment-based trigger where no separation minima apply.

items.question_text: If traffic you have issued is not reported in sight, controllers must inform the pilot when that traffic is no factor or no longer observed on radar.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph requires a follow-up when previously issued traffic is not reported in sight and later becomes no factor or no longer observed.

items.question_text: If a pilot requests radar vectors to assist in avoiding traffic, controllers must provide them when the aircraft is within their area of jurisdiction or coordination has been effected with the sector or facility whose area the aircraft is operating in, and if unable they must inform the pilot.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The vector-assistance provision is conditional on jurisdiction or coordination, and inability must be communicated.

items.question_text: This traffic-advisory rule applies differently to radar-identified aircraft, non-radar-identified aircraft, and Mode C aircraft that are not radar identified, with content tailored to the information available in each case.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph has separate advisory content rules for those categories.

items.situation: A radar-identified aircraft asks for vectors to avoid traffic you just issued. The aircraft is outside your area of jurisdiction and you have not coordinated with the sector that owns it. Another controller says you should vector the aircraft anyway and explain the coordination later if needed.
items.para_context: When requested, issue radar vectors to assist in avoiding traffic only if the aircraft is within your jurisdiction or coordination has been effected with the controlling sector or facility. If unable to provide radar vectors, inform the pilot.

choices.text: Do not issue the vector until jurisdiction or coordination exists; if unable, tell the pilot you are unable radar vectors.
Issue the vector immediately because any traffic-avoidance request automatically overrides jurisdiction and coordination limits.
Stay silent because the advisory already discharged your duty once the traffic call was made.
Switch the aircraft to the next frequency and let the receiving sector decide whether the original vector would have been safe.

items.explanation: The paragraph authorizes traffic-avoidance vectors only under the listed jurisdiction or coordination conditions.

items.situation: Issue a radar traffic advisory for a Boeing 727 at 11 o'clock, 10 miles, southbound, converging, at 17,000 feet. Build the advisory.
items.target_phrase: TRAFFIC ELEVEN O CLOCK TEN MILES SOUTHBOUND CONVERGING BOEING SEVEN TWENTY SEVEN ONE SEVEN THOUSAND

items.question_text: When should advisory information about UAS activity be issued?

choices.text: When UAS activity is known, pilot-reported, or tower-observed and in your judgment the proximity warrants the advisory.
Only after a UAS causes an actual conflict with another aircraft.
Only when the UAS is operating above 400 feet AGL.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for unmanned traffic reports by name.

items.explanation: Known, pilot-reported, and tower-observed UAS activity can all trigger the advisory when proximity warrants it.

items.question_text: For pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS activity, controllers must continue issuing advisories to potentially impacted aircraft for at least 15 minutes following the last report.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The 15-minute continuation rule applies after the last pilot-reported or tower-observed report.

items.question_text: If UAS activity is known, controllers should include position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude when those details are known and proximity warrants the advisory.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the listed details for known UAS activity when available.

items.question_text: This UAS-advisory rule applies to known activity and to pilot-reported or tower-observed activity, with slightly different detail sets for each category.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Known activity and reported or observed activity are both covered, but the listed advisory details differ slightly.

items.situation: A quadcopter is at 12 o'clock, 1 mile, southbound, 400 feet and below. Build the UAS advisory.
items.target_phrase: U A S ACTIVITY TWELVE O CLOCK ONE MILE SOUTHBOUND QUAD COPTER FOUR HUNDRED FEET AND BELOW

items.question_text: When should radio communications normally be transferred?
items.explanation: The transfer occurs before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated or specified.

choices.text: Before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated or specified.
Only after the aircraft is already inside the receiving controller's area.
Only after the pilot requests the new frequency.
Only after the aircraft has completed every altitude change in the sector.

items.question_text: Which frequency information may sometimes be omitted during a communications transfer?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists omission cases such as FSS frequency, previously given or SID-published departure frequency, and certain terminal ground or local frequencies.

choices.text: Items such as an FSS frequency, a previously given or SID-published departure frequency, and certain terminal ground or local frequencies under the listed conditions.
Any center frequency whenever the aircraft is already in radar contact.
All frequencies preceding a decimal point, regardless of bandwidth or service type.
The facility name whenever the frequency is stated in full.

items.question_text: What is considered a reasonable amount of time for taking action to establish or restore expected communications?
items.explanation: The note defines it as 5 minutes from area entry or coming within radio coverage.

choices.text: 5 minutes from the time the aircraft enters the controller's area of jurisdiction or comes within radio coverage.
10 minutes from the last radar handoff.
Until the next sector reports a concern.
Only after the pilot misses two separate calls on frequency.

items.question_text: In an operationally advantageous situation, after coordination with the receiving controller, controllers may instruct aircraft on the ground to monitor the receiving controller's frequency.
items.explanation: That monitor option is specifically authorized in subparagraph e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When should controllers avoid issuing a frequency change to certain helicopters?
items.explanation: The paragraph singles out helicopters known to be single-piloted during air-taxiing, hovering, or low-level flight.

choices.text: When the helicopter is known to be single-piloted and is air-taxiing, hovering, or in low-level flight.
Whenever any helicopter is above 3,000 feet AGL in cruise flight.
Whenever a helicopter is flying IFR, regardless of pilot workload or altitude.
Only when the receiving controller has not yet turned on a second VHF transmitter.

items.question_text: Which phraseology is used when the controller does not want the pilot to change frequency but expects the pilot may want one?
items.explanation: The phraseology is REMAIN THIS FREQUENCY.

choices.text: REMAIN THIS FREQUENCY

items.question_text: How may a controller approve an operational request if the readback would otherwise be lengthy?
items.explanation: The phraseology APPROVED AS REQUESTED may be substituted in lieu of a lengthy readback.

choices.text: By using the phrase APPROVED AS REQUESTED.
By saying STAND BY until the requester assumes the approval.
By omitting any verbal response and making the operational change directly.
By saying ONLY IF PRACTICABLE instead of approved.

items.question_text: What should follow a restricted approval of an operational request?
items.explanation: Restrictions are stated first, followed by the word APPROVED.

choices.text: State the restrictions and then the word APPROVED.
State the word APPROVED first and omit the restrictions unless the requester objects.
State STAND BY because all restricted approvals must be delayed approvals.
State UNABLE because any restriction converts the approval into a denial.

items.question_text: What does STAND BY mean in response to an operational request?
items.explanation: Stand by is neither approval nor denial; it acknowledges the request and promises a later response.

choices.text: It is not an approval or denial; it acknowledges the request and means the controller will respond later.
It means the request is approved pending no further objections.
It means the request is denied without further discussion.
It means the requester must restate the request in longer form.

items.question_text: If time permits and you cannot approve a request, what may accompany the word unable?
items.explanation: A reason and or additional instructions may follow unable.

choices.text: A reason and or additional instructions.
Only the word approved as requested.
Only a frequency change to another controller.
Nothing; unable must never be explained.

items.question_text: STAND BY is an approval that allows the requester to proceed unless told otherwise later.
items.explanation: The note explicitly says stand by is not an approval or denial.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When are wake turbulence procedures applied?
items.explanation: They apply when an aircraft is operating behind another aircraft and wake turbulence separation is required.

choices.text: When an aircraft is operating behind another aircraft and wake turbulence separation is required.
Whenever two aircraft are on the same frequency.
Only after a pilot reports a wake encounter.
Only during approach control operations.

items.question_text: How long must wake turbulence separation minima continue for IFR aircraft that are not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation?
items.explanation: They must continue to touchdown.

choices.text: To touchdown.
Only to the final approach fix.
Only until the aircraft is established on final.
Only until the runway is in sight.

items.question_text: Wake turbulence separation minima continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.
items.explanation: That is the paragraph's direct rule.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which aircraft are excluded from the touchdown continuation rule in 2-1-19?
items.explanation: The exception category is IFR aircraft making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: IFR aircraft making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.
All VFR aircraft behind any heavy aircraft, regardless of wake separation requirements.
Only helicopters operating behind fixed-wing traffic.
Only aircraft landing at non-towered airports.

items.question_text: What is the basic trigger for using wake turbulence procedures at all?
items.explanation: The trigger is operation behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required.

choices.text: Operation behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required.
Any trailing operation in IMC regardless of the aircraft involved.
Any operation following a crossing runway departure.
Any operation when a heavy or super aircraft is somewhere on the airport.

items.question_text: When should wake turbulence cautionary advisories be issued to VFR aircraft not being radar vectored behind larger traffic?
items.explanation: That terminal VFR condition is one of the specifically listed advisory cases.

choices.text: When they are operating behind an aircraft that requires wake turbulence separation.
Only after the VFR pilot reports feeling a wake encounter.
Only if the larger traffic is a super aircraft, not a heavy aircraft.
Only if the VFR aircraft has accepted visual separation from the heavier traffic.

items.question_text: Which IFR case specifically requires a wake turbulence cautionary advisory?
items.explanation: IFR aircraft accepting a visual approach or visual separation are specifically listed.

choices.text: When an IFR aircraft accepts a visual approach or visual separation.
Whenever an IFR aircraft is still on instruments behind traffic.
Only when the IFR aircraft requests the advisory by name.
Only during radar departure sequencing.

items.question_text: How should the traffic description change when the traffic is known to be Super or Heavy?
items.explanation: The cautionary advisory includes the word Super or Heavy as appropriate.

choices.text: Include the word Super for super aircraft and Heavy for heavy aircraft.
Use only the aircraft type designator; the words Super and Heavy are not part of wake cautionary advisories.
Use Super only for civil traffic and Heavy only for military traffic.
Include those words only if the pilot asks whether the traffic is large.

items.question_text: Controllers must issue cautionary information to any aircraft if, in the controller's opinion, wake turbulence may have an adverse effect on it.
items.explanation: That broad judgment-based caution rule is stated directly in subparagraph b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is the prescribed cautionary wake phraseology?
items.explanation: The phraseology is CAUTION WAKE TURBULENCE followed by traffic information.

choices.text: CAUTION WAKE TURBULENCE

items.question_text: When are traffic advisories omitted by rule rather than controller judgment?
items.explanation: They are omitted when the aircraft is operating in Class A airspace or when omission is requested by the pilot.

choices.text: When the aircraft is operating in Class A airspace or omission is requested by the pilot.
Whenever the aircraft is VFR outside Class B airspace.
Whenever the pilot has Mode C but is not radar identified.
Whenever the controller believes traffic calls might be distracting.

items.question_text: How is the position of traffic normally described to a radar-identified aircraft?
items.explanation: Normally it is given in terms of the 12-hour clock, with the cardinal-direction method used when rapidly maneuvering traffic prevents accurate clock-code traffic.

choices.text: By azimuth in terms of the 12-hour clock, or by the eight cardinal compass points when rapidly maneuvering traffic prevents accurate clock-code issuance.
By runway-relative left or right only.
Always by cardinal compass points; the clock method is used only for non-radar traffic.
By exact latitude and longitude coordinates in every case.

items.question_text: What should you tell the pilot if requested radar vectors for traffic avoidance cannot be provided?
items.explanation: If unable to provide vectors, inform the pilot that you are unable radar vectors.

choices.text: Inform the pilot that you are unable radar vectors.
Say nothing further, because traffic advisories do not require a response when vectors cannot be given.
Issue the vector anyway if the pilot insists, even without jurisdiction or coordination.
Transfer the aircraft to another sector without explanation.

items.question_text: When traffic issued earlier is not reported in sight, what follow-up may be required later?
items.explanation: The pilot is informed when the traffic becomes no factor or is no longer observed.

choices.text: Tell the pilot the traffic is no factor or no longer observed.
Restate the original traffic call every minute until the pilot reports visual contact.
Assume the pilot now sees the traffic once radar range begins to increase.
Discontinue all traffic advisories to that aircraft for the rest of the flight.

items.question_text: How is traffic described to a non-radar-identified aircraft?
items.explanation: Non-radar traffic calls use distance and direction from a fix, direction of flight, type and altitude if known, and ETA over the fix if appropriate.

choices.text: By distance and direction from a fix, direction of flight, and if known type, altitude, and ETA over the fix when appropriate.
Only by clock position and mileage because fix-based calls are reserved for radar traffic.
Only by the other aircraft's destination airport and route string.
Only by assigning a temporary holding fix to the traffic and reading back its beacon code.

items.question_text: Which phrase may be used when previously issued traffic is no factor?
items.explanation: The phraseology includes TRAFFIC NO FACTOR.

choices.text: TRAFFIC NO FACTOR

items.question_text: When should an advisory about UAS activity be issued?
items.explanation: The advisory is issued for known, pilot-reported, or tower-observed UAS activity when the proximity warrants it in the controller's judgment.

choices.text: When known, pilot-reported, or tower-observed UAS activity is close enough that the controller judges the advisory is warranted.
Only after a UAS has caused an actual loss of separation.
Only when the UAS is above 400 feet and therefore automatically requires priority handling.
Only when a pilot asks specifically for drone-related traffic calls.

items.question_text: Which information is included for known UAS activity if it is known?
items.explanation: Known UAS activity includes position, distance, course, type of UA, and altitude when known.

choices.text: Position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude.
Only the registration number and the remote pilot's contact information.
Only the exact latitude and longitude, regardless of whether distance or course are easier to understand.
Only position and altitude, because type and course are never included in UAS advisories.

items.question_text: How long are advisories to potentially impacted aircraft continued after the last pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS report?
items.explanation: The continuation period is at least 15 minutes.

choices.text: At least 15 minutes.
At least 5 minutes.
At least 30 minutes.
Until the next controller assumes jurisdiction, regardless of time.

items.question_text: For pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS activity, the advisory should, if known, include position, altitude, course, and type.
items.explanation: That is the listed detail set for the reported or observed category.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What type of UAS was used in one of the example advisories?
items.explanation: One example specifically used a quad copter.

choices.text: Quad copter.
Blimp.
Gyroplane.
Tiltrotor transport.
